solution:
We will prove the claim assuming only that the convex hull of the
points $(a_{i}, b_{i})$ contains the origin in its interior. (Thanks
to Marshall Buck for pointing out that the last three words are
necessary in the previous sentence!) Let $u = \log x, v = \log
y$ so that the left-hand side of the given equation is
\begin{multline}
(a_1, b_1) \exp(a_1 u + b_1 v) + (a_2, b_2) \exp(a_2 u + b_2 v) + \\
\cdots + (a_n, b_n) \exp(a_n u + b_n v).
\end{multline}
Now note that (1) is the gradient of the function
\begin{gather*}
f(u,v) = exp(a_1 u + b_1 v) +
exp(a_2 u + b_2 v) + \\
\cdots + exp(a_n u + b_n v),
\end{gather*}
and so it suffices to show $f$ has a critical point. We will in fact
show $f$ has a global minimum.

Clearly we have
\[
f(u,v) \geq \exp\left( \max_i (a_i u + b_i v) \right).
\]
Note that this maximum is positive for $(u,v) \neq (0,0)$:  if we had
$a_i u + b_i v < 0$ for all $i$, then the subset $ur + vs < 0$ of the
$rs$-plane would be a half-plane containing all of the points $(a_i,
b_i)$, whose convex hull would then not contain the origin, a
contradiction.

The function $\max_{i} (a_{i}u + b_{i}v)$ is clearly
continuous on the unit circle $u^{2} + v^{2} = 1$, which is compact.
Hence it has a global minimum $M > 0$, and so for all $u,v$,
\[
\max_{i} (a_{i} u + b_{i} v) \geq M \sqrt{u^{2} + v^{2}}.
\]
In particular, $f \geq n+1$ on the disk of radius $\sqrt{(n+1)/M}$.
Since $f(0,0) = n$, the infimum of $f$ is the same over the entire
$uv$-plane as over this disk, which again is compact.
Hence $f$ attains its infimal value at some point in the disk,
which is the desired global minimum.

Noam Elkies has suggested an alternate solution as follows: for $r >
0$, draw the loop traced by (1) as $(u,v)$ travels
counterclockwise around the circle $u^2 + v^2 = r^2$. For $r=0$, this
of course has winding number 0 about any point, but for $r$ large, one
can show this loop has winding number 1 about the origin, so somewhere in
between the loop must pass through the origin. (Proving this latter
fact is a little tricky.)

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long.solution:
We will prove the claim assuming only that the convex hull of the
points $(coeffaidx, coeffbidx)$ contains the origin in its interior. (Thanks
to Marshall Buck for pointing out that the last three words are
necessary in the previous sentence!) Let $loguvar = \log varxpos, logvvar = \log
varypos$ so that the left-hand side of the given equation is
\begin{multline}
(coeffaone, coeffbone) \exp(coeffaone loguvar + coeffbone logvvar) + (coeffatwo, coeffbtwo) \exp(coeffatwo loguvar + coeffbtwo logvvar) + \\
\cdots + (coeffanmax, coeffbnmax) \exp(coeffanmax loguvar + coeffbnmax logvvar).
\end{multline}
Now note that (1) is the gradient of the function
\begin{gather*}
f(loguvar,logvvar) = \exp(coeffaone loguvar + coeffbone logvvar) +
\exp(coeffatwo loguvar + coeffbtwo logvvar) + \\
\cdots + \exp(coeffanmax loguvar + coeffbnmax logvvar),
\end{gather*}
and so it suffices to show $f$ has a critical point. We will in fact
show $f$ has a global minimum.

Clearly we have
\[
f(loguvar,logvvar) \geq \exp\left( \max_i (coeffaidx loguvar + coeffbidx logvvar) \right).
\]
Note that this maximum is positive for $(loguvar,logvvar) \neq (0,0)$:  if we had
$coeffaidx loguvar + coeffbidx logvvar < 0$ for all $i$, then the subset $loguvar radialvr + logvvar slopevar < 0$ of the
$radialvr slopevar$-plane would be a half-plane containing all of the points $(coeffaidx,
coeffbidx)$, whose convex hull would then not contain the origin, a
contradiction.

The function $\max_{i} (coeffaidx loguvar + coeffbidx logvvar)$ is clearly
continuous on the unit circle $loguvar^{2} + logvvar^{2} = 1$, which is compact.
Hence it has a global minimum $minbound > 0$, and so for all $loguvar,logvvar$,
\[
\max_{i} (coeffaidx loguvar + coeffbidx logvvar) \geq minbound \sqrt{loguvar^{2} + logvvar^{2}}.
\]
In particular, $f \geq verticesn+1$ on the disk of radius $\sqrt{(verticesn+1)/minbound}$.
Since $f(0,0) = verticesn$, the infimum of $f$ is the same over the entire
loguvar logvvar-plane as over this disk, which again is compact.
Hence $f$ attains its infimal value at some point in the disk,
which is the desired global minimum.

Noam Elkies has suggested an alternate solution as follows: for $radialvr >
0$, draw the loop traced by (1) as $(loguvar,logvvar)$ travels
counterclockwise around the circle $loguvar^{2} + logvvar^{2} = radialvr^{2}$. For $radialvr=0$, this
of course has winding number 0 about any point, but for $radialvr$ large, one
can show this loop has winding number 1 about the origin, so somewhere in
between the loop must pass through the origin. (Proving this latter
fact is a little tricky.)

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We will prove the claim assuming only that the convex hull of the
points $(eclipseeach, nebulaeach)$ contains the origin in its interior. (Thanks
to Marshall Buck for pointing out that the last three words are
necessary in the previous sentence!) Let $breadcrumb = \log lighthouse, paintbrush = \log
snowflake$ so that the left-hand side of the given equation is
\begin{multline}
(eclipseone, nebulaone) \exp(eclipseone \, breadcrumb + nebulaone \, paintbrush) + (eclipsetwo, nebulatwo) \exp(eclipsetwo \, breadcrumb + nebulatwo \, paintbrush) + \\
\cdots + (eclipsezen, nebulazen) \exp(eclipsezen \, breadcrumb + nebulazen \, paintbrush).
\end{multline}
Now note that (1) is the gradient of the function
\begin{gather*}
f(breadcrumb,paintbrush) = \exp(eclipseone \, breadcrumb + nebulaone \, paintbrush) +
\exp(eclipsetwo \, breadcrumb + nebulatwo \, paintbrush) + \\
\cdots + \exp(eclipsezen \, breadcrumb + nebulazen \, paintbrush),
\end{gather*}
and so it suffices to show $f$ has a critical point. We will in fact
show $f$ has a global minimum.

Clearly we have
\[
f(breadcrumb,paintbrush) \geq \exp\left( \max_i (eclipseeach \, breadcrumb + nebulaeach \, paintbrush) \right).
\]
Note that this maximum is positive for $(breadcrumb,paintbrush) \neq (0,0)$:  if we had
$eclipseeach \, breadcrumb + nebulaeach \, paintbrush < 0$ for all $i$, then the subset $breadcrumb \, cloudscape + paintbrush \, moonstone < 0$ of the
$cloudscape moonstone$-plane would be a half-plane containing all of the points $(eclipseeach,
nebulaeach)$, whose convex hull would then not contain the origin, a
contradiction.

The function $\max_{i} (eclipseeach \, breadcrumb + nebulaeach \, paintbrush)$ is clearly
continuous on the unit circle $breadcrumb^{2} + paintbrush^{2} = 1$, which is compact.
Hence it has a global minimum $starflower > 0$, and so for all $breadcrumb,paintbrush$,
\[
\max_{i} (eclipseeach \, breadcrumb + nebulaeach \, paintbrush) \geq starflower \sqrt{breadcrumb^{2} + paintbrush^{2}}.
\]
In particular, $f \geq driftwood+1$ on the disk of radius $\sqrt{(driftwood+1)/starflower}$.
Since $f(0,0) = driftwood$, the infimum of $f$ is the same over the entire
$breadcrumb paintbrush$-plane as over this disk, which again is compact.
Hence $f$ attains its infimal value at some point in the disk,
which is the desired global minimum.

Noam Elkies has suggested an alternate solution as follows: for $cloudscape >
0$, draw the loop traced by (1) as $(breadcrumb,paintbrush)$ travels
counterclockwise around the circle $breadcrumb^{2} + paintbrush^{2} = cloudscape^{2}$. For $cloudscape=0$, this
of course has winding number 0 about any point, but for $cloudscape$ large, one
can show this loop has winding number 1 about the origin, so somewhere in
between the loop must pass through the origin. (Proving this latter
fact is a little tricky.)

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $(verticalone, horizontalone), (verticaltwo, horizontaltwo), \ldots, (verticaln, horizontaln)$ be the vertices of a
convex polygon which contains the origin in its interior. Prove that
there exist positive real numbers $verticalcoor$ and $horizontalcoor$ such that
\begin{gather*}
(verticalone, horizontalone)verticalcoor^{verticalone} horizontalcoor^{horizontalone} + (verticaltwo, horizontaltwo)verticalcoor^{verticaltwo}horizontalcoor^{horizontaltwo} + \cdots \\
+ (verticaln, horizontaln)verticalcoor^{verticaln}horizontalcoor^{horizontaln} = (0,0).
\end{gather*}
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We will prove the claim assuming only that the convex hull of the
points $(verticali, horizontali)$ contains the origin in its interior. (Thanks
to Marshall Buck for pointing out that the last three words are
necessary in the previous sentence!) Let $compression = \log verticalcoor,\; stretching = \log
horizontalcoor$ so that the left-hand side of the given equation is
\begin{multline}
(verticalone, horizontalone) \exp(verticalone compression + horizontalone stretching) + (verticaltwo, horizontaltwo) \exp(verticaltwo compression + horizontaltwo stretching) + \\
\cdots + (verticaln, horizontaln) \exp(verticaln compression + horizontaln stretching).
\end{multline}
Now note that (1) is the gradient of the function
\begin{gather*}
f(compression,stretching) = exp(verticalone compression + horizontalone stretching) +
exp(verticaltwo compression + horizontaltwo stretching) + \\
\cdots + exp(verticaln compression + horizontaln stretching),
\end{gather*}
and so it suffices to show $f$ has a critical point. We will in fact
show $f$ has a global minimum.

Clearly we have
\[
f(compression,stretching) \geq \exp\left( \max_i (verticali compression + horizontali stretching) \right).
\]
Note that this maximum is positive for $(compression,stretching) \neq (0,0)$:  if we had
$verticali compression + horizontali stretching < 0$ for all $i$, then the subset $compression diameter + stretching stillness < 0$ of the
$diameter stillness$-plane would be a half-plane containing all of the points $(verticali,
horizontali)$, whose convex hull would then not contain the origin, a
contradiction.

The function $\max_{i} (verticali compression + horizontali stretching)$ is clearly
continuous on the unit circle $compression^{2} + stretching^{2} = 1$, which is compact.
Hence it has a global minimum $globalmax > 0$, and so for all $compression,stretching$,
\[
\max_{i} (verticali compression + horizontali stretching) \geq globalmax \sqrt{compression^{2} + stretching^{2}}.
\]
In particular, $f \geq continuum+1$ on the disk of radius $\sqrt{(continuum+1)/globalmax}$.
Since $f(0,0) = continuum$, the infimum of $f$ is the same over the entire
compressionstretching-plane as over this disk, which again is compact.
Hence $f$ attains its infimal value at some point in the disk,
which is the desired global minimum.

Noam Elkies has suggested an alternate solution as follows: for $diameter >
0$, draw the loop traced by (1) as $(compression,stretching)$ travels
counterclockwise around the circle $compression^2 + stretching^2 = diameter^2$. For $diameter=0$, this
of course has winding number 0 about any point, but for $diameter$ large, one
can show this loop has winding number 1 about the origin, so somewhere in
between the loop must pass through the origin. (Proving this latter
fact is a little tricky.)

garbled_string.solution:
We will prove the claim assuming only that the convex hull of the
points $(kqjtmzre, tpvdzwla)$ contains the origin in its interior. (Thanks
to Marshall Buck for pointing out that the last three words are
necessary in the previous sentence!) Let $mclpzrqe = \log qzxwvtnp, nskdwtuy = \log
hjgrksla$ so that the left-hand side of the given equation is
\begin{multline}
(zvxcrlpe, gfjqdmzo) \exp(zvxcrlpe mclpzrqe + gfjqdmzo nskdwtuy) + (bjkstemq, wlyhkpra) \exp(bjkstemq mclpzrqe + wlyhkpra nskdwtuy) + \\
\cdots + (lsdowprm, crmsqvtx) \exp(lsdowprm mclpzrqe + crmsqvtx nskdwtuy).
\end{multline}
Now note that (1) is the gradient of the function
\begin{gather*}
f(mclpzrqe,nskdwtuy) = \exp(zvxcrlpe mclpzrqe + gfjqdmzo nskdwtuy) +
\exp(bjkstemq mclpzrqe + wlyhkpra nskdwtuy) + \\
\cdots + \exp(lsdowprm mclpzrqe + crmsqvtx nskdwtuy),
\end{gather*}
and so it suffices to show $f$ has a critical point. We will in fact
show $f$ has a global minimum.

Clearly we have
\[
f(mclpzrqe,nskdwtuy) \geq \exp\left( \max_i (kqjtmzre mclpzrqe + tpvdzwla nskdwtuy) \right).
\]
Note that this maximum is positive for $(mclpzrqe,nskdwtuy) \neq (0,0)$:  if we had
$kqjtmzre mclpzrqe + tpvdzwla nskdwtuy < 0$ for all $i$, then the subset $mclpzrqe fjgkrnsa + nskdwtuy plhmdzqo < 0$ of the
$fjgkrnsa plhmdzqo$-plane would be a half-plane containing all of the points $(kqjtmzre,
 tpvdzwla)$, whose convex hull would then not contain the origin, a
contradiction.

The function $\max_{i} (kqjtmzre mclpzrqe + tpvdzwla nskdwtuy)$ is clearly
continuous on the unit circle $mclpzrqe^{2} + nskdwtuy^{2} = 1$, which is compact.
Hence it has a global minimum $hrfbwzeq > 0$, and so for all $mclpzrqe,nskdwtuy$,
\[
\max_{i} (kqjtmzre mclpzrqe + tpvdzwla nskdwtuy) \geq hrfbwzeq \sqrt{mclpzrqe^{2} + nskdwtuy^{2}}.
\]
In particular, $f \geq xrcpsgqa+1$ on the disk of radius $\sqrt{(xrcpsgqa+1)/hrfbwzeq}$.
Since $f(0,0) = xrcpsgqa$, the infimum of $f$ is the same over the entire
$mclpzrqe nskdwtuy$-plane as over this disk, which again is compact.
Hence $f$ attains its infimal value at some point in the disk,
which is the desired global minimum.

Noam Elkies has suggested an alternate solution as follows: for $fjgkrnsa >
0$, draw the loop traced by (1) as $(mclpzrqe,nskdwtuy)$ travels
counterclockwise around the circle $mclpzrqe^2 + nskdwtuy^2 = fjgkrnsa^2$. For $fjgkrnsa=0$, this
of course has winding number 0 about any point, but for $fjgkrnsa$ large, one
can show this loop has winding number 1 about the origin, so somewhere in
between the loop must pass through the origin. (Proving this latter
fact is a little tricky.)

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 3$ and let $K\subset\mathbb R^{d}$ be a compact set whose convex hull ${\operatorname{conv}}\,K$ contains the origin in its interior.  

Let $\mu$ be a finite positive Borel measure on $K$ whose support is all of $K$ (equivalently, $\mu$ is not supported by any proper affine subspace).

For $u\in\mathbb R^{d}$ define  
\[
Z(u)=\int_{K}e^{\langle v,u\rangle}\,d\mu(v),\qquad
G(u)=\int_{K}v\,e^{\langle v,u\rangle}\,d\mu(v),\qquad
\Sigma(u)=\int_{K}vv^{\top}e^{\langle v,u\rangle}\,d\mu(v).
\]
(Here $A^{\top}$ denotes the transpose of a matrix $A$ and $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$ is the Euclidean inner product.)

Prove the following assertions.

1. (Central parameter)  
   There exists a unique $u^{\ast}\in\mathbb R^{d}$ such that $G(u^{\ast})=0$.

2. (Strict positivity of the covariance at $u^{\ast}$)  
   $\Sigma(u^{\ast})$ is positive-definite.

3. (Global parametrisation of the interior of the polytope)  
   The map  
   \[
   \Phi:=\nabla\log Z:\mathbb R^{d}\longrightarrow{\operatorname{int}}\bigl({\operatorname{conv}}\,K\bigr),
   \qquad 
   u\longmapsto\frac{G(u)}{Z(u)},
   \]
   is a $\mathcal C^{\infty}$-diffeomorphism of $\mathbb R^{d}$ onto $\operatorname{int}(\operatorname{conv}K)$.  
   Equivalently, for every $p\in\operatorname{int}(\operatorname{conv}K)$ there exists a unique $u(p)\in\mathbb R^{d}$ such that  
   \[
   \int_{K}v\,e^{\langle v,u(p)\rangle}\,d\mu(v)=p\,Z\bigl(u(p)\bigr).
   \]

4. (The covariance map need not be injective)  
   Give an explicit example showing that  
   \[
   C:\mathbb R^{d}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Sym}^{+}_{d},\qquad 
   u\longmapsto\Sigma(u),
   \]
   is not injective in general.  (Hint: take $d=3$, $K$ the vertices of the cube $\{\pm1\}^{3}$, and $\mu$ the uniform measure.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we use the notation introduced in the statement, write $f:=\log Z$ and $\|\cdot\|$ for the Euclidean norm.

Step 0.  Elementary properties.  
Because $K$ is compact and $\mu$ is finite, $Z,G,\Sigma$ are finite-valued and $\mathcal C^{\infty}$ on $\mathbb R^{d}$, and $Z(u)>0$ for every $u$.


Step 1.  Coercivity of $f$.  
Since $0\in{\operatorname{int}}({\operatorname{conv}}\,K)$, there exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that for every unit vector $\xi$ one can find $v_{\xi}\in K$ with $\langle v_{\xi},\xi\rangle\ge\varepsilon$.  Hence, for $t\ge0$,
\[
Z(t\xi)\;\ge\;e^{t\varepsilon}\,\mu\!\bigl(\{v\in K:\langle v,\xi\rangle\ge\varepsilon\}\bigr)\;\xrightarrow[t\to\infty]{}\;\infty,
\]
and therefore $f(u)=\log Z(u)\xrightarrow[\|u\|\to\infty]{}\infty$.  Thus $f$ is coercive and attains at least one global minimum.


Step 2.  Strict convexity of $f$.  
Differentiating,
\[
\nabla f(u)=\frac{G(u)}{Z(u)},\qquad
\nabla^{2}f(u)=\frac{\Sigma(u)}{Z(u)}-\frac{G(u)G(u)^{\top}}{Z(u)^{2}}
          =\operatorname{Cov}_{\mu_{u}}(v),
\]
where $\mu_{u}$ is the probability measure with density
$d\mu_{u}(v)=e^{\langle v,u\rangle}d\mu(v)/Z(u)$.  
Because $\operatorname{supp}\mu_{u}=K$ is full-dimensional and $\mu_{u}$ is not supported by any proper affine subspace, the variance of $\langle w,v\rangle$ under $\mu_{u}$ is positive for every $0\ne w$, whence $\nabla^{2}f(u)$ is positive-definite.  Thus $f$ is strictly convex and its global minimiser is unique.


Step 3.  The central parameter $u^{\ast}$.  
Let $u^{\ast}$ be the unique minimiser of $f$.  The first-order condition $\nabla f(u^{\ast})=0$ is exactly $G(u^{\ast})=0$, proving Item 1.


Step 4.  Positive-definiteness of $\Sigma(u^{\ast})$.  
Since $G(u^{\ast})=0$,
\[
\nabla^{2}f(u^{\ast})=\frac{\Sigma(u^{\ast})}{Z(u^{\ast})}.
\]
The left-hand side is positive-definite (Step 2) and $Z(u^{\ast})>0$, so $\Sigma(u^{\ast})$ is positive-definite.  Item 2 is established.


Step 5.  $\Phi$ is a global $\mathcal C^{\infty}$-diffeomorphism.

5a.  $\Phi$ is a local diffeomorphism.  
Because $\nabla^{2}f(u)$ is positive-definite everywhere, the Jacobian $D\Phi(u)=\nabla^{2}f(u)$ is invertible for every $u$.  The inverse-function theorem yields that $\Phi$ is a local $\mathcal C^{\infty}$-diffeomorphism.

5b.  Injectivity of $\Phi$.  
For $u_{1}\ne u_{2}$ strict convexity of $f$ gives
\[
\langle\Phi(u_{1})-\Phi(u_{2}),\,u_{1}-u_{2}\rangle
=\langle\nabla f(u_{1})-\nabla f(u_{2}),\,u_{1}-u_{2}\rangle>0,
\]
so $\Phi(u_{1})=\Phi(u_{2})$ would imply $u_{1}=u_{2}$.  Thus $\Phi$ is injective.

5c.  Surjectivity onto ${\operatorname{int}}({\operatorname{conv}}\,K)$ via Legendre-Fenchel duality.  

(i)  The Legendre conjugate.  
Define the convex conjugate of $f$,
\[
f^{\ast}(p):=\sup_{u\in\mathbb R^{d}}\bigl(\langle p,u\rangle-f(u)\bigr),
\qquad p\in\mathbb R^{d}.
\]
Because $f$ is finite everywhere and strictly convex, $f^{\ast}$ is a proper, lower semicontinuous, strictly convex function.

(ii)  Identification of $\operatorname{dom}f^{\ast}$.  
Let $h_{K}$ be the support function of $K$,
\[
h_{K}(\xi):=\max_{v\in K}\langle v,\xi\rangle,\qquad\xi\in\mathbb R^{d}.
\]
Compactness of $K$ yields $|h_{K}(\xi)|\le\|K\|_{\infty}\|\xi\|$ and
\[
f(u)=\log\!\int_{K}e^{\langle v,u\rangle}\,d\mu(v)
        \le h_{K}(u)+\log\mu(K)\qquad(u\in\mathbb R^{d}).
\]
If $p\notin{\operatorname{conv}}\,K$, Hahn-Banach separation provides $\xi$ with $\langle p,\xi\rangle>h_{K}(\xi)$.  Taking $u=t\xi$ and $t\to\infty$ gives
\[
f^{\ast}(p)\ge\langle p,t\xi\rangle-f(t\xi)
            \ge t\bigl(\langle p,\xi\rangle-h_{K}(\xi)\bigr)-\log\mu(K)
            \xrightarrow[t\to\infty]{}\infty,
\]
so $f^{\ast}(p)=\infty$.  Conversely, if $p\in{\operatorname{conv}}\,K$, then $\langle p,u\rangle\le h_{K}(u)$ for all $u$, whence $f^{\ast}(p)\le\log\mu(K)<\infty$.  Thus
\[
{\operatorname{dom}}f^{\ast}={\operatorname{conv}}\,K,\qquad 
{\operatorname{int}}({\operatorname{dom}}f^{\ast})={\operatorname{int}}({\operatorname{conv}}\,K).
\]

(iii)  Legendre type of $f$.  
The function $f$ is $\mathcal C^{1}$ on all of $\mathbb R^{d}$ and strictly convex, and its gradient blows up only at infinity (there is no boundary to its effective domain).  Hence $f$ is a \emph{Legendre} (sometimes called \emph{essentially smooth and essentially strictly convex}) function in the sense of Rockafellar, Theorem 26A.

(iv)  Global bijectivity of the gradient.  
For Legendre functions one has (Rockafellar, Theorem 26.5):
\[
\nabla f:\mathbb R^{d}\longrightarrow{\operatorname{int}}\bigl({\operatorname{dom}}f^{\ast}\bigr)
\quad\text{is a}\;\mathcal C^{\infty}\text{-diffeomorphism}.
\]
Because ${\operatorname{dom}}f^{\ast}={\operatorname{conv}}\,K$, this gives
\[
\Phi=\nabla f:\mathbb R^{d}\longrightarrow{\operatorname{int}}\bigl({\operatorname{conv}}\,K\bigr)
\quad\text{is onto}.
\]
Combining surjectivity with 5a and 5b we obtain that $\Phi$ is a global $\mathcal C^{\infty}$-diffeomorphism, completing Item 3.


Step 6.  A non-injective covariance map (Item 4).  
Take $d=3$, $K=\{\pm1\}^{3}$ (the $8$ vertices of the unit cube) and let $\mu$ be the uniform measure (mass $1/8$ at each vertex).  Writing $u=(u_{1},u_{2},u_{3})$,
\[
Z(u)=8\prod_{j=1}^{3}\cosh u_{j}.
\]
Because $\cosh$ is an even function, $\Sigma(-u)=\Sigma(u)$ for every $u$.  For $u\ne0$ we have $u\ne -u$, yet $C(u)=C(-u)$.  Hence the covariance map $C:u\mapsto\Sigma(u)$ is not injective.


All four assertions are therefore proved.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension and continuum:  The original statement dealt with finitely many points in two or three dimensions; the enhanced problem handles {\em arbitrary} dimension $d\ge3$ and a {\em continuum} of points via an integral over a Borel measure.  
• Multiple objectives:  Not only must one find $u$ with vanishing first moment, one must also prove uniqueness, global diffeomorphism properties, and the realisation of {\em every} admissible covariance matrix.  
• Advanced tools:  The solution invokes strict convexity of log-Laplace transforms, coercivity arguments, the inverse-function theorem, proper maps, covering-space theory, and a touch of degree theory—well beyond the elementary gradient-vanishing argument of the original.  
• Interacting concepts:  Convex geometry, differential topology, and probability (moments and covariance) interact intricately.  
These additions raise the problem far above the complexity of both the original and the previous kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 3$ and let $K\subset\mathbb R^{d}$ be a compact set whose convex hull ${\operatorname{conv}}\,K$ contains the origin in its interior.  

Let $\mu$ be a finite positive Borel measure on $K$ whose support is all of $K$ (equivalently, $\mu$ is not supported by any proper affine subspace).

For $u\in\mathbb R^{d}$ define  
\[
Z(u)=\int_{K}e^{\langle v,u\rangle}\,d\mu(v),\qquad
G(u)=\int_{K}v\,e^{\langle v,u\rangle}\,d\mu(v),\qquad
\Sigma(u)=\int_{K}vv^{\top}e^{\langle v,u\rangle}\,d\mu(v).
\]
(Here $A^{\top}$ denotes the transpose of a matrix $A$ and $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$ is the Euclidean inner product.)

Prove the following assertions.

1. (Central parameter)  
   There exists a unique $u^{\ast}\in\mathbb R^{d}$ such that $G(u^{\ast})=0$.

2. (Strict positivity of the covariance at $u^{\ast}$)  
   $\Sigma(u^{\ast})$ is positive-definite.

3. (Global parametrisation of the interior of the polytope)  
   The map  
   \[
   \Phi:=\nabla\log Z:\mathbb R^{d}\longrightarrow{\operatorname{int}}\bigl({\operatorname{conv}}\,K\bigr),
   \qquad 
   u\longmapsto\frac{G(u)}{Z(u)},
   \]
   is a $\mathcal C^{\infty}$-diffeomorphism of $\mathbb R^{d}$ onto $\operatorname{int}(\operatorname{conv}K)$.  
   Equivalently, for every $p\in\operatorname{int}(\operatorname{conv}K)$ there exists a unique $u(p)\in\mathbb R^{d}$ such that  
   \[
   \int_{K}v\,e^{\langle v,u(p)\rangle}\,d\mu(v)=p\,Z\bigl(u(p)\bigr).
   \]

4. (The covariance map need not be injective)  
   Give an explicit example showing that  
   \[
   C:\mathbb R^{d}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Sym}^{+}_{d},\qquad 
   u\longmapsto\Sigma(u),
   \]
   is not injective in general.  (Hint: take $d=3$, $K$ the vertices of the cube $\{\pm1\}^{3}$, and $\mu$ the uniform measure.)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we use the notation introduced in the statement, write $f:=\log Z$ and $\|\cdot\|$ for the Euclidean norm.

Step 0.  Elementary properties.  
Because $K$ is compact and $\mu$ is finite, $Z,G,\Sigma$ are finite-valued and $\mathcal C^{\infty}$ on $\mathbb R^{d}$, and $Z(u)>0$ for every $u$.


Step 1.  Coercivity of $f$.  
Since $0\in{\operatorname{int}}({\operatorname{conv}}\,K)$, there exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that for every unit vector $\xi$ one can find $v_{\xi}\in K$ with $\langle v_{\xi},\xi\rangle\ge\varepsilon$.  Hence, for $t\ge0$,
\[
Z(t\xi)\;\ge\;e^{t\varepsilon}\,\mu\!\bigl(\{v\in K:\langle v,\xi\rangle\ge\varepsilon\}\bigr)\;\xrightarrow[t\to\infty]{}\;\infty,
\]
and therefore $f(u)=\log Z(u)\xrightarrow[\|u\|\to\infty]{}\infty$.  Thus $f$ is coercive and attains at least one global minimum.


Step 2.  Strict convexity of $f$.  
Differentiating,
\[
\nabla f(u)=\frac{G(u)}{Z(u)},\qquad
\nabla^{2}f(u)=\frac{\Sigma(u)}{Z(u)}-\frac{G(u)G(u)^{\top}}{Z(u)^{2}}
          =\operatorname{Cov}_{\mu_{u}}(v),
\]
where $\mu_{u}$ is the probability measure with density
$d\mu_{u}(v)=e^{\langle v,u\rangle}d\mu(v)/Z(u)$.  
Because $\operatorname{supp}\mu_{u}=K$ is full-dimensional and $\mu_{u}$ is not supported by any proper affine subspace, the variance of $\langle w,v\rangle$ under $\mu_{u}$ is positive for every $0\ne w$, whence $\nabla^{2}f(u)$ is positive-definite.  Thus $f$ is strictly convex and its global minimiser is unique.


Step 3.  The central parameter $u^{\ast}$.  
Let $u^{\ast}$ be the unique minimiser of $f$.  The first-order condition $\nabla f(u^{\ast})=0$ is exactly $G(u^{\ast})=0$, proving Item 1.


Step 4.  Positive-definiteness of $\Sigma(u^{\ast})$.  
Since $G(u^{\ast})=0$,
\[
\nabla^{2}f(u^{\ast})=\frac{\Sigma(u^{\ast})}{Z(u^{\ast})}.
\]
The left-hand side is positive-definite (Step 2) and $Z(u^{\ast})>0$, so $\Sigma(u^{\ast})$ is positive-definite.  Item 2 is established.


Step 5.  $\Phi$ is a global $\mathcal C^{\infty}$-diffeomorphism.

5a.  $\Phi$ is a local diffeomorphism.  
Because $\nabla^{2}f(u)$ is positive-definite everywhere, the Jacobian $D\Phi(u)=\nabla^{2}f(u)$ is invertible for every $u$.  The inverse-function theorem yields that $\Phi$ is a local $\mathcal C^{\infty}$-diffeomorphism.

5b.  Injectivity of $\Phi$.  
For $u_{1}\ne u_{2}$ strict convexity of $f$ gives
\[
\langle\Phi(u_{1})-\Phi(u_{2}),\,u_{1}-u_{2}\rangle
=\langle\nabla f(u_{1})-\nabla f(u_{2}),\,u_{1}-u_{2}\rangle>0,
\]
so $\Phi(u_{1})=\Phi(u_{2})$ would imply $u_{1}=u_{2}$.  Thus $\Phi$ is injective.

5c.  Surjectivity onto ${\operatorname{int}}({\operatorname{conv}}\,K)$ via Legendre-Fenchel duality.  

(i)  The Legendre conjugate.  
Define the convex conjugate of $f$,
\[
f^{\ast}(p):=\sup_{u\in\mathbb R^{d}}\bigl(\langle p,u\rangle-f(u)\bigr),
\qquad p\in\mathbb R^{d}.
\]
Because $f$ is finite everywhere and strictly convex, $f^{\ast}$ is a proper, lower semicontinuous, strictly convex function.

(ii)  Identification of $\operatorname{dom}f^{\ast}$.  
Let $h_{K}$ be the support function of $K$,
\[
h_{K}(\xi):=\max_{v\in K}\langle v,\xi\rangle,\qquad\xi\in\mathbb R^{d}.
\]
Compactness of $K$ yields $|h_{K}(\xi)|\le\|K\|_{\infty}\|\xi\|$ and
\[
f(u)=\log\!\int_{K}e^{\langle v,u\rangle}\,d\mu(v)
        \le h_{K}(u)+\log\mu(K)\qquad(u\in\mathbb R^{d}).
\]
If $p\notin{\operatorname{conv}}\,K$, Hahn-Banach separation provides $\xi$ with $\langle p,\xi\rangle>h_{K}(\xi)$.  Taking $u=t\xi$ and $t\to\infty$ gives
\[
f^{\ast}(p)\ge\langle p,t\xi\rangle-f(t\xi)
            \ge t\bigl(\langle p,\xi\rangle-h_{K}(\xi)\bigr)-\log\mu(K)
            \xrightarrow[t\to\infty]{}\infty,
\]
so $f^{\ast}(p)=\infty$.  Conversely, if $p\in{\operatorname{conv}}\,K$, then $\langle p,u\rangle\le h_{K}(u)$ for all $u$, whence $f^{\ast}(p)\le\log\mu(K)<\infty$.  Thus
\[
{\operatorname{dom}}f^{\ast}={\operatorname{conv}}\,K,\qquad 
{\operatorname{int}}({\operatorname{dom}}f^{\ast})={\operatorname{int}}({\operatorname{conv}}\,K).
\]

(iii)  Legendre type of $f$.  
The function $f$ is $\mathcal C^{1}$ on all of $\mathbb R^{d}$ and strictly convex, and its gradient blows up only at infinity (there is no boundary to its effective domain).  Hence $f$ is a \emph{Legendre} (sometimes called \emph{essentially smooth and essentially strictly convex}) function in the sense of Rockafellar, Theorem 26A.

(iv)  Global bijectivity of the gradient.  
For Legendre functions one has (Rockafellar, Theorem 26.5):
\[
\nabla f:\mathbb R^{d}\longrightarrow{\operatorname{int}}\bigl({\operatorname{dom}}f^{\ast}\bigr)
\quad\text{is a}\;\mathcal C^{\infty}\text{-diffeomorphism}.
\]
Because ${\operatorname{dom}}f^{\ast}={\operatorname{conv}}\,K$, this gives
\[
\Phi=\nabla f:\mathbb R^{d}\longrightarrow{\operatorname{int}}\bigl({\operatorname{conv}}\,K\bigr)
\quad\text{is onto}.
\]
Combining surjectivity with 5a and 5b we obtain that $\Phi$ is a global $\mathcal C^{\infty}$-diffeomorphism, completing Item 3.


Step 6.  A non-injective covariance map (Item 4).  
Take $d=3$, $K=\{\pm1\}^{3}$ (the $8$ vertices of the unit cube) and let $\mu$ be the uniform measure (mass $1/8$ at each vertex).  Writing $u=(u_{1},u_{2},u_{3})$,
\[
Z(u)=8\prod_{j=1}^{3}\cosh u_{j}.
\]
Because $\cosh$ is an even function, $\Sigma(-u)=\Sigma(u)$ for every $u$.  For $u\ne0$ we have $u\ne -u$, yet $C(u)=C(-u)$.  Hence the covariance map $C:u\mapsto\Sigma(u)$ is not injective.


All four assertions are therefore proved.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension and continuum:  The original statement dealt with finitely many points in two or three dimensions; the enhanced problem handles {\em arbitrary} dimension $d\ge3$ and a {\em continuum} of points via an integral over a Borel measure.  
• Multiple objectives:  Not only must one find $u$ with vanishing first moment, one must also prove uniqueness, global diffeomorphism properties, and the realisation of {\em every} admissible covariance matrix.  
• Advanced tools:  The solution invokes strict convexity of log-Laplace transforms, coercivity arguments, the inverse-function theorem, proper maps, covering-space theory, and a touch of degree theory—well beyond the elementary gradient-vanishing argument of the original.  
• Interacting concepts:  Convex geometry, differential topology, and probability (moments and covariance) interact intricately.  
These additions raise the problem far above the complexity of both the original and the previous kernel variant.
